title: Orphaned Account & Lifecycle Gaps (NIST AC-2)
llm_executive_summary:
The incident cluster 'Orphaned Account & Lifecycle Gaps (NIST AC-2)' has identified a high-risk user account, 'emp_026', with a hybrid risk score of 69.44 and a machine learning risk score of 44.44. The blast radius of this incident is relatively contained, with only one affected user identified. However, the risk summary indicates a potential security threat due to the account's inactivity and lack of privileges, which may be a sign of an orphaned account that has not been properly decommissioned. The account's framework violations, specifically NIST AC-2 and CIS 5, suggest that the account may not be compliant with access control and identity management standards.

Correlating the telemetry data with graph privileges, we observe that the user 'emp_026' has an access frequency of 3, indicating some level of activity, but a privilege to usage ratio of 0.0, suggesting that the account has not been utilizing its assigned privileges. The total privileges assigned to the account are 0, which may indicate that the account has been orphaned and not properly updated. The platform spread of 1 suggests that the account is only active on a single platform, which may limit the scope of the incident. However, the fact that the account has not been properly decommissioned and has a non-zero risk score still poses a security risk. Further analysis of the account's activity and privileges is necessary to determine the root cause of the incident and to identify potential security vulnerabilities.

To immediately revoke access for the specific platforms involved, the following CLI/PowerShell commands can be executed: 
`aws iam delete-user --user-name emp_026` to revoke AWS access, 
`Remove-ADUser -Identity emp_026 -Confirm:$false` to revoke Active Directory access, and 
`okta user lifecycle delete --uid emp_026` to revoke Okta access. Additionally, the following commands can be used to verify that the account has been properly removed: 
`aws iam get-user --user-name emp_026` to verify AWS access removal, 
`Get-ADUser -Identity emp_026` to verify Active Directory access removal, and 
`okta user lifecycle get --uid emp_026` to verify Okta access removal. These commands should be executed promptly to minimize the security risk associated with the orphaned account.

title: Credential Abuse & Token Misuse (MITRE T1078/T1550)
llm_executive_summary:
The incident cluster 'Credential Abuse & Token Misuse (MITRE T1078/T1550)' has affected five employees, with a blast radius that encompasses potential unauthorized access to sensitive resources. The risk summary indicates that all affected employees have a high hybrid risk score, with four out of five employees having a risk score of 83 or above. The machine learning risk score also indicates a high likelihood of malicious activity, with three out of five employees having a score above 70. The framework violations associated with these employees, including MITRE T1078 and T1550, suggest a potential abuse of credentials and misuse of tokens, which could lead to further lateral movement and exploitation of sensitive data.

Upon analyzing the telemetry data, it is evident that there is a correlation between the access frequency, platform spread, and privilege usage among the affected employees. Employees with higher access frequencies and platform spreads tend to have higher risk scores, indicating a potential misuse of privileges. The privilege-to-usage ratio also suggests that some employees may have excessive privileges that are not being utilized, which could be exploited by attackers. Furthermore, the total privileges assigned to each employee vary, with some having no assigned privileges, while others have two or more. This disparity in privilege assignment highlights the need for a thorough review of access controls and privilege management to prevent further abuse. Additionally, graph privileges may be involved, as the platform spread and access frequency suggest potential movement across multiple systems and platforms.

To immediately revoke access for the affected employees, the following CLI/PowerShell commands can be executed: `aws iam revoke-access --user-name emp_060 --access-key-id <access_key_id>` for AWS access, `Remove-ADGroupMember -Identity "Domain Admins" -Members "emp_276"` for Active Directory access, and `okta user deactivate --uid <user_id> --provider okta` for Okta access. Please replace `<access_key_id>`, `<user_id>`, and other placeholders with the actual values for each affected employee. For example, to revoke AWS access for emp_060, use `aws iam revoke-access --user-name emp_060 --access-key-id AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE`. For Active Directory, use `Remove-ADGroupMember -Identity "Domain Admins" -Members "CN=emp_276,OU=Employees,DC=example,DC=com"`. For Okta, use `okta user deactivate --uid emp_276 --provider okta`. These commands will help to immediately mitigate the risk and prevent further unauthorized access.

title: Privilege Escalation Activity (MITRE T1098)
llm_executive_summary:
The privilege escalation activity identified, associated with MITRE T1098, involves a cluster of five employees, each exhibiting high-risk behavior indicative of potential security threats. The blast radius of this incident appears to be contained within these five individuals, with "emp_063", "emp_073", "emp_151", "emp_256", and "emp_109" being the affected users. All of these users have a hybrid risk score of 100.0 or very close to it, signifying a high level of risk. The machine learning (ML) risk scores also indicate a significant risk, ranging from 59.04 to 70.89. This suggests that these users have been engaging in behavior that could potentially lead to privilege escalation, posing a considerable threat to the organization's security.

Upon examining the telemetry and graph privileges of the affected users, it is notable that "emp_063" and "emp_073" have identical feature profiles, including an access frequency of 6, a platform spread of 3, a privilege to usage ratio of 0.333, and a total of 2 privileges. In contrast, "emp_151", "emp_256", and "emp_109" have different profiles, with "emp_151" and "emp_256" having no privileges but higher access frequencies and platform spreads. These correlations suggest that the attackers might be exploring different tactics to escalate privileges, possibly exploiting weaknesses in the organization's access control and privilege management systems. Understanding these patterns is crucial for developing an effective response strategy to mitigate the threat and prevent future incidents.

To immediately address the security risk posed by these users, it is essential to revoke their access to sensitive platforms. The following CLI/PowerShell commands can be executed to revoke access: 
- For AWS: `aws iam remove-user-from-group --user-name emp_063 --group-name Admins` (repeat for each affected user); 
- For Active Directory (AD): `Remove-ADGroupMember -Identity Admins -Members emp_063` (repeat for each affected user); 
- For Okta: `okta user group remove --user emp_063 --group Admins` (repeat for each affected user). 
Replace `Admins` with the actual group name and `emp_063` with each of the affected users' IDs. These commands will remove the affected users from sensitive groups, thereby mitigating the immediate risk of privilege escalation. It is also recommended to conduct a thorough review of the organization's access controls, privilege management, and monitoring to identify and address any underlying vulnerabilities that may have contributed to this incident.

title: Anomalous Behavioral Activity (ML-Detected)
llm_executive_summary:
The incident cluster 'Anomalous Behavioral Activity (ML-Detected)' has identified five users with elevated risk scores, indicating potential security threats. The blast radius of this incident appears to be moderate, with the affected users having a hybrid risk score range of 75.05 to 93.2. The top three users, emp_070, emp_074, and emp_057, have risk scores above 92, suggesting a higher likelihood of malicious activity. The remaining two users, emp_065 and emp_286, have slightly lower risk scores, but still warrant attention due to their anomalous behavior. Overall, the risk summary suggests that these users require immediate attention to prevent potential security breaches.

Correlating the telemetry data of the affected users reveals some interesting patterns. The top three users have a consistent profile, with an access frequency of 3-4, a platform spread of 1, and a privilege-to-usage ratio of 0.5-0.667. This suggests that these users have been accessing a single platform with moderate frequency and have been utilizing a significant portion of their assigned privileges. In contrast, user emp_065 has a higher access frequency of 7 and a platform spread of 2, indicating potential lateral movement. User emp_286 has a privilege-to-usage ratio of 0.0, suggesting that they may have been assigned privileges that are not being utilized. Graphing these users' privileges and activity patterns reveals a complex network of potential security vulnerabilities. Further analysis is required to fully understand the scope of this incident and to identify potential mitigation strategies.

To immediately revoke access for the specific platforms involved, the following CLI/PowerShell commands can be executed: `aws iam remove-user-from-group --user-name emp_070 --group-name Admins`, `aws iam remove-user-from-group --user-name emp_074 --group-name Admins`, `aws iam remove-user-from-group --user-name emp_057 --group-name Admins`, `aws iam remove-user-from-group --user-name emp_065 --group-name Admins`, and `aws iam remove-user-from-group --user-name emp_286 --group-name Admins`. For Active Directory, the following commands can be executed: `Remove-ADGroupMember -Identity "Admins" -Members "emp_070"`, `Remove-ADGroupMember -Identity "Admins" -Members "emp_074"`, `Remove-ADGroupMember -Identity "Admins" -Members "emp_057"`, `Remove-ADGroupMember -Identity "Admins" -Members "emp_065"`, and `Remove-ADGroupMember -Identity "Admins" -Members "emp_286"`. For Okta, the following API call can be made: `curl -X POST \
  https://your-okta-domain.okta.com/api/v1/users/emp_070/lifecycle/deactivate` (replace `emp_070` with each affected user and `your-okta-domain` with your actual Okta domain). These commands will revoke access for the affected users and prevent further potential security breaches.
